narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the strength and determination it takes to pursue your dreams in a field that needs more voices like yours. Your application highlighted a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20, an LSAT score of 35, upper-middle family income, full-time student status, and school deciles of 4 in Year 1 and 3 in Year 3—credentials that show real effort amid your unique background. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 41.9% chance you'll face failure on that critical test, tipping the scales toward rejection to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction wasn't random; it stemmed from weighing your profile against typical applicants, where averages include LSAT scores around 36.7, undergraduate GPAs near 3.21, Year 1 deciles of 5.71, and Year 3 deciles of 5.53. Your LSAT score had the strongest positive sway toward predicting success, pulling the odds in your favor more than any other factor, closely followed by your undergraduate GPA, which also nudged the model toward expecting you to pass. On the flip side, your lower Year 3 school decile stood out as the biggest push toward the failure prediction, suggesting a dip in performance relative to peers that raised concerns. Smaller influences included your family's upper-middle income slightly tilting toward higher risk and your full-time status offering a minor boost, while your Year 1 decile provided a subtle positive note. Compared to most students—92.7% of whom are full-time like you—your academic trajectory in later years deviated from the stronger upward trends that typically signal bar success. This holistic view led to the tough call, but it's not the end of your story. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting those later-year grades and retaking the LSAT if possible, while seeking mentorship programs tailored for Black women in law to build an even more compelling profile. You've already overcome so much—keep pushing, because your potential shines through, and the right opportunity awaits. We're rooting for you.
